speaker: interviewer
text: Who was paying your bills? Your food? How were you getting money for these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. The first job that I got in Bolivia was translator, interpreter, for the Bolivian army, which I thought would pay my bills. It didn't, because after four weeks, I found out that they told me that they had not any-- they did not have any budgetary appropriations for the job. So I worked for nothing. Then I opened up a translator bureau in Bolivia, which was all right. I helped-- I also translated and interpreted. I helped the Jewish community, because sometimes immigrants got into legal difficulties, and I was able to interpret for them and help them out their difficulties. I knew some Bolivian officials, so that worked out all right. But then later on, I got employed by a British firm again, import/export. Then things got very, very tough, politically. Bolivia was a country of great interest to the Axis powers because it had a lot of raw materials, tin and everything else. And they were very friendly-- the government people-- towards Germany. The Germans had been there 100 years before, and they liked the Germans. They were a little bit anti-American-- imperialism and what have you. And they were fertile ground for Nazi infiltrators and Nazi agents who were trying to create little problems for the Americans to divert their attention. After 1940, a group formed of Jewish people, and also non-Jews, who had fled Germany, to fight the German influence in Bolivia, and to also try to expose the German embassy in Bolivia as the center of Nazi espionage. We were able to do that, because the postmaster who was in Bolivia, La Paz, was on our side and a little bit of a, shall we say, realistic remuneration for his services was available.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we found out that the-- how the German embassy was trying to contact Bolivians, working with Bolivian people there in order to subvert the Allied effort, in order to create a revolution on the fascist side, Peron-type, like Peron in Argentina. That was-- that tape-- we worked with British and the Americans. That-- that tape, plus copies of the correspondence, was sent to the embassies of Great Britain and the United States, who in turn contacted the foreign ministry in Bolivia. They sent the ambassador of Germany and Italy packing, broke off relations with Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I had met a very interesting person who was a local representative of UPI, United Press International. He told me one day, he said, you know, you are a-- you-- you have a lot of resources here. You speak Spanish, you-- and English. You know the people here. You have friends. You can be of great help in the war effort. I said, there isn't any war. Well, he said, there will be war pretty soon, between the United States and Japan. Things were already brewing. That was in October, November 1941, shortly before Pearl Harbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, would you mind working for the American embassy? I said, as a former German stateless person? He said, we can take care of that. So he arranged for a conference interview by the ambassador, for an interview of myself by the American ambassador, Mr. de Boal. I had this interview, and I was employed. I was hired as translator-dash-economic advisor, analyst. I said, but how are you going to get me OKed by the White House? In the meantime, Pearl Harbor had occurred. He said, well, we'll ask the White House for special permission to employ you, which they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my job was very interesting. I had to give the embassy a report on socioeconomic conditions in Bolivia, how Bolivia was, with 95% of people living in abject misery and the rest having everything. It was a fertile ground for discontent and for all sorts of political problems. And I gave them-- I gave information based on interviews that I had with labor leaders, with other people-- also those who were close to Argentina and knew what the Argentinians had in mind for Bolivia. And that proved very interesting. I also said that in about six or eight months, I was told, that there would be a-- a group of officers, army officers, who would stage a revolt in Bolivia-- the typical Peron-type revolt. The ambassador sort of ridiculed it, and it didn't go to Washington. And that-- the event that I had predicted occurred six or eight months later. And then other things-- I had to-- we also had to do one thing. We had to give reports on merchandise which was transshipped by German firms in Argentina which was meant for Germany and Italy, but which was addressed to Argentinian firms, who-- which in turn did the transshipment, all of this. We were fully aware of what was going on. I also had to give reports on the economic situation. Because at that time, Bolivia-- America had to institute export controls, and everything that went out of the United States, exported, had to get export approval. And we had to file a report with Washington on the firms who applied for the export controls, and lots of them were speculators. And I-- I was in charge of that program. Well, in the meantime, I had the privilege of meeting some very high US officials at meetings. One was Henry Wallace, who was the vice president of the United States at the time, who came. Very enlightened man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you supporting your whole family at this point? Your in-laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- no, my family. My in-laws were sort of self-supporting. My father-in-law had gotten a job in the shipping department of a big Bolivian firm. His Spanish was poor, but he was a good worker, and so he supported himself. And we lived together. We had moved together. My-- my family, we had-- in the meantime, we got our first child, Gabi, who you will see in a few minutes. She was born in Bolivia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? What day?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- she was born on January 1, 1942. So her birthday will always be a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we left Bolivia in 1945, she was just barely three years old. My mother was there, too, of course. My mother was supported by us. And so we lived very happily together, although living conditions in Bolivia were anything but satisfactory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, why did you want to leave Bolivia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wanted to leave Bolivia, A, because it offered no future. There was no future. There was-- in a-- in a country where most of the people have nothing and where the most primitive needs had to be imported, where you could see, every day, the dogs scrambling for food with the poorest of the poor Indians, the public dump. And where people died in the mines, in the coal mines and in the tin mines, like flies. And I was there just about two months. I served-- I didn't mention that-- as-- as the-- the assistant to the administrator at one tin mine. And I saw how the Indians lived. That is not a country which has a great future, at least not for our children. And we had always wanted to go to the United States. My-- my late wife's grandfather had come to the United States in the '60s and '70s, last century.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come to the West Coast on a wagon, and told his family about Los Angeles and San Francisco. And so we wanted to go to the United States. And the climate in Bolivia was impossible, practically. I mean, my wife suffered from it. So everything drew us to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, in 1940-- end of '44, when the end of the war was in sight, the ambassador told me, I'm going to be going to work, back to Washington, very soon. Is there anything I can do for you? I said, yes, help me come to the United States. He said, I will. So he appeared before Army-Navy Board. During the war there was no immigration here to the United States. And every applicant had to be represented before the Army-Navy Board. And they asked him-- he told me later on-- why we want to come to the United States. And he said, well, for the same reason that your forefathers came here-- for the privilege of becoming American citizens.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me-- he got me my visas. He sponsored our visas. I got the highest civil service rating- I have the book here-- 'Excellent.' And that is how our family left Bolivia with our visas in 1945, to come to the United States. My in-laws were included.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we arrived in-- in Miami, Florida, after a very adventurous journey. Because there was no-- practically no shipping, passenger shipping facilities, during the war. So we had to take it in stages. First, from Bolivia to Ecuador, we took a little boat which was a cattle boat. . We were on top. It was in August. Ah, you can imagine the conditions. It was anything but Chanel No. 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: From Ecuador, we went up to Colombia. From Colombia, we went to-- from southern Colombia, we went up to Barranquilla. And from Barranquilla, we hopped over to Miami. In Miami, I had a job offer. But after three weeks in Miami in June, I decided this is not where I would like to stay. We went on to Fort Worth, Texas, by rail. At that time-- it was in 1945, June, July, 1945. The Pacific theater was the main focus of the war effort, because Germany and Italy had already surrendered. And everything moved. You see-- you saw troops on all of these-- on all of the trains moving west, and you had to get priorities to get on a train. Finally, Fort Worth was not the city that I wanted to stay in, because it was-- I figured I would like to go to work for an import-export firm. I could have gone to Washington, diplomatic career, but my wife said, finally, we are going to stay put somewhere. And she was-- Ursula was actually right. Her advice, in many situations in my life, has helped us. We stayed. We then-- we decided to go to the West Coast. The lure of the West Coast, which had become a tradition in my wife's family-- Los Angeles, California, was what attracted us. And so we landed here on the 22nd or 23rd of June, 1945. Whole family-- lock, stock and barrel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --do first?

speaker: interviewee
text: First my wife, Ursula, had a cousin here who's still alive. She lives in Arizona. She had come her with her husband in 1939 as a refugee. We stayed with her for one night. And then we went to a boarding house. At that time, it was during the war. You couldn't rent anything, except at tremendous cost, because there was no-- there had been no new housing constructed during the war. All civilian building had ceased. There was OPA, Office of Price Administration. All prices were frozen. So there was no incentive for people to rent. You had to-- you could either buy, or you could rent, but you had to buy some useless furniture for a tremendously high price, circumventing the regulations. We-- and no hotels would take you over three days. You had to move out after three days. We found a little boarding house where we could have-- we had breakfast and dinner. And it was overlooking MacArthur Park on West Sixth Street. The Leighton Hotel, at the time. We were very happy to be there. We stayed there for three months. We picnicked on Westlake Park on Sunday. We figured we were-- we were really leading the life of Riley. I found a job. My-- I found a job after about a week. I went from bank to bank, trying to find a job in the international banking department. Because international trade was something that I thought I knew something about. And after about a week, I was offered a little job by Union Bank, which is still in business. At that time, it was at Eighth and Hill Streets. Ursula went to take care of a boy for-- with a family, a Jewish family in Boyle Heights. My mother-in-law worked as practical nurse for an old gentleman who was a cripple. And she was a frail little woman-- how she handled that physically, I don't know, but she had tremendous willpower. My father-in-law became a shipping clerk in one of the manufacturing companies of ceramics-- which was the article at that time in California, ceramic products for families. And so I worked-- I stayed with the bank for two years, then was hired by a steel company, one of the leading steel companies, here, owned by Jews on the West Coast. Became first assistant to the import-export manager, then export manager. I worked on the marketing. I traveled to Latin America, to Japan. In the meantime, I heard that one could-- one could study law, I could study law-- I was always interested in the law-- by taking correspondence course-- correspondence courses. I enrolled with La Salle Extension University in Chicago, and I started studying law. And after a number of years where I always had my books with me-- I studied nights, I studied weekends, and during trips, I always had my law books. And we're taking, right now, some very gigantic steps here. I'm just telling you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took the California bar, passed it, and went into the practice of law. 1962. First, I practiced law in the evenings. I drew will for people-- wills for people. I did certain things. But then I figured the only way to practice law is to take the jump, which I did, hoping I was-- I felt like the-- the parachutist who hopes that the parachute will open. Well, it finally opened shortly before landing. And so I landed smoothly, and I practiced international law. I worked for several consulates, foreign clients trying to establish themselves in California and vice versa. I also work a great deal, do a great deal, in international arbitration. And I'm very much interested in Jewish affairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Ursula work during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Ursula helped me tremendously. Without her help, take all-- freeing me from all the problems of household, all the decisions, and enabling me to pursue my law studies in addition to making a living-- that was invaluable. Without her, I wouldn't be where I am right now. She stuck to me in every situation of my life. She worked, too. She worked then as a clerk for a bank. She worked for a little while for Bank of America, and then for one of the clothiers' firm here as a bookkeeper. And then we had our second child, Leslie, who was born about 16 and 1/2 years after the birth of our first child. Both children have made us very happy. And then Ursula didn't have to work anymore. We moved to Westwood. We had lived in Hollywood for quite some time. And she helped me make social arrangement, meet clients socially. She worked for the community here, particularly in the public school. She was one of the direct-- for two terms, a director of the PTA of Uni High, and Emerson High before. And she had-- she also helped me with a program trying to give-- show hospitality to foreign visitors here in Los Angeles. Not only, not limited only to my practice, but generally. I then became also a-- I became the president of the Foreign Trade Association of Southern California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: I've been active in-- yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- in part of what you did, you worked with a number of foreign governments that have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --commended you in a number of ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did those relationships come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was very interesting. Because my first relationship was with a German Consulate General. There, I had tremendous obstacles, emotional obstacles. Because right after the war, after we heard about what happened in Europe, we almost swore that we would never have-- want to have anything to do with Germans anymore. Time went by, and in 1962, 1963, I was approached by a young attache to the German Consulate General here who had heard about me, knowing that I spoke German, whether they could recommend my services to people here, to Germans who needed American legal assistance, and whether they could also retain me as their lawyer. And I told him what my problem was. He said, I am of the young generation. I said-- he said, I do not feel guilty for what my parents did, but it is so abhorrent to me that I can assure you we will-- we know we young Germans have a responsibility for the future. And very slowly, bridges were built, and I talked to these people. I found that they were-- most of them, at least, wanted to start over a new leaf, a new book, as far as Germany was concerned. I had some very open dialogues with them. And yes, I became legal counsel to the German Consulate General, then later on to the French Consulate General, to the Austrian Consulate General. In fact, the one who made me, appointed me as the Austrian-- as a legal counsel to the Austrian Consulate General is the current president of Austria, Thomas Klestil, with whom I have had a friendship ever since, and who has invited me to come to Vienna. Also with the Italians, the Italian Consulate General. And I have decorations from all of these governments for service to them, loyal service to them. In addition to that, I-- I represent most European governments here. And that's my current status. Unfortunately, coming back to my personal life, my lovely wife Ursula passed away in November 1989, after two years' battle against cancer. Two years, I was on my own. I was by myself. And then I-- I learned that it would be very hard for me to live alone, and I had the good fortune of meeting Birgitta, my current wife, my present wife, who has given me a lovely new home, and to whom I got married over two years ago. I have the great fortune of having not only two lovely children, daughters and sons-in-law, but also four grandchildren-- three of whom-- three of whom, in fact, are the children of Gabi's and Eli's. That's Danny, Michal, Tamar, and on the other side Leslie, who gave me a granddaughter less than a year ago, Julia. And they all live-- my children all live, fortunately, in Los Angeles. And I feel I am really blessed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? I don't know. I'm not only a survivor, but I think that I have been privileged. And to be able to tell the story, to be able to make a new life for myself, and really, for ourselves, in this beautiful country of ours. And I can really say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I want to just ask you a couple of questions--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about some of the things you mentioned. When you were in Bolivia, and you gained an audience with the president, and he said, give-- essentially, took you to the secretary and said, give me the names of the people you want, how did you know where to stop the list of names?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could-- well, I should have added. He said, your family. Because I was talking to him about my mother, my in-laws, and Ursula. He said-- I mentioned other relatives of mine who unfortunately perished. He said, I cannot do any more than that. He said, otherwise I will get into difficulty. And so-- [SIGH]-- he decided-- it was decided these are the three.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, if you remember when you were-- it was Kristallnacht, and you were escaping to the train, the last train that was not searched by the Gestapo, and you passed that mob that was attacking people that you knew, did-- what were the thoughts that were crossing your mind as you saw-- as you saw that scene?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I saw that mob, I figured, this is it. I'm-- no doubt they're going to arrest me and throw me into jail, or send me to concentration camp, what have you. I kept going. And all of a sudden, it was just like a miracle had happened. They went past me. They did. Nobody stopped me. And still, I thought-- I couldn't comprehend what had happened. Then I went to the station. I thought maybe they have barriers right there. They have some checkpoints. I went right through. It was just like a miracle happened. Another miracle happened to me when I was shipped back from Holland to Germany. I was able to talk myself out of-- out of the-- going to concentration camp. And then, I must say, a very-- one of the most moving events when we were on-board the vessel, the first Friday evening services on the vessel. It felt almost like we had left Mitzrayim. We had a young rabbi who went to Chile with his family. He held services for us on Shabbat morning. And he was talking about going to an uncertain future, like the Israelites of thousands of years ago, and that we have escaped the hell of Germany-- that we will make our way in the new country, whatever may be awaiting us. It was-- we almost felt that we had been saved by a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very powerful. Did you have any thoughts as you saw the Torah-- excuse me-- the synagogue burning in Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When I saw that happen, I had the feeling that was one of the greatest tragedy [BACKROUND NOISE] since the destruction of the temple. And I must say, speaking of that, when I mentioned that I felt blessed, I want to add one thing. My generation has seen two things happen-- the worst misfortune that befell the Jewish people since the destruction of the temple, but then less than 10 years later, the realization of the 2,000-year-old dream, the reestablishment of the Jewish state in Israel. How providence, fate, or God works, we don't know. We can't understand him, or it. And I feel we cannot judge God. There's so many things we don't know. But to have been blessed with, A, saving my own family, leading a productive life here, and witnessing a 2,000-year-old dream come true, is something that very few people, except the members of my generation, have seen or will see. The Chinese say, when they want to curse somebody, may he live in interesting times. I have lived in interesting times, but I don't feel cursed.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop right here. And why don't we start the next tape with some people, your family members?
